thesis: EXH1.DE (European Oil & Gas) is the clearest regime trade: best 5d (+1.9%) and 20d (+5.6%) relative strength in the candidate set, above both SMA20 and SMA50, with the macro thesis fully aligned — the oil supply shock makes energy the highest-conviction sector rotation this week. Slightly tighter stop given established trend, wider take profit to capture the oil momentum leg.
outcome_notes: Price drifted in the right direction but the oil momentum leg never accelerated — a scheduled exit rather than a take-profit hit signals the supply-shock catalyst was either already priced or failed to reprice within the holding window. The above-SMA positioning held and the trade closed green, but the move was consistent with noise rather than a regime trend.
risks_observed: Single-sector energy concentration meant any intraday reversal in oil sentiment (OPEC commentary, demand revision) would have flipped this to a loss with minimal warning. The asymmetry of a tighter stop against a wider take-profit was not rewarded here, and the scheduled exit left potential upside on the table had momentum arrived later in the session.

thesis: EXV3.DE (European Technology) is flagging a breakout: volume 3.69x the 20-day average is the strongest flow signal in the set today, price is above both SMAs, RSI at 61 with positive MACD histogram confirms the setup; the AI capex cycle remains intact per the macro view, making this a technically-confirmed entry into the second-ranked macro sector.
outcome_notes: The volume spike did not produce price follow-through — exit below entry on a scheduled close suggests the 3.69x volume bar was more likely absorption or distribution than genuine accumulation, invalidating the breakout read at entry. RSI and MACD confirmed momentum at the time but were lagging the actual flow dynamic.
risks_observed: Elevated volume without same-session price confirmation is a known false-breakout pattern; the signal weighting should distinguish between demand-driven and supply-driven volume spikes. Running this alongside EXH1.DE created a two-leg basket where a broad risk-off move would have hit both simultaneously, and with neither stop triggered on the day, full notional was exposed throughout — no safety net fired.

thesis: The 5d HY-IG outperformance (+38bp) confirms this is a pure duration selloff, not a credit-stress event — HY spreads are stable to tightening, which directly benefits IHYG.L. It is the sole candidate above both SMA20 and SMA50 with positive MACD and RSI 52, making it the only technically-valid bond expression in the current rising-rate, no-recession regime.
outcome_notes: Price was flat entry-to-exit at $0.91, producing a near-zero loss attributable to friction rather than adverse price movement. The scheduled exit fired before any meaningful directional move developed, so the thesis — that stable HY spreads in a duration-led selloff would lift IHYG.L — was neither confirmed nor refuted; the hold period was simply too short to observe the expected spread-compression tailwind.
risks_observed: Single-name concentration with no paired IG short left the position fully exposed to any abrupt credit-risk repricing that would have invalidated the HY-IG divergence premise mid-hold. The scheduled exit mechanism, while clean, removed the possibility of riding a delayed thesis realisation and also means there is no price-action evidence to validate or challenge the technical setup for future entries.

thesis: Oil supply shock confirmed by IEA (commercial inventories depleting rapidly with weeks left) and corroborated by price action well above SMA20 ($138.57) and SMA50 ($128.01) with RSI 61.6. Iran deal risk is real but incomplete — US proposal is a temporary OFAC waiver, Iran demands full sanctions removal — leaving supply-tightness thesis intact for today's session.
outcome_notes: Price moved cleanly from entry to exit without meaningful retracement, consistent with sustained supply-tightness rather than a spike-and-fade pattern. The scheduled exit captured a 1.46% move; no adverse intraday reversal forced early closure, suggesting the IEA inventory signal held through the session. Exit was time-driven rather than thesis-invalidating, so the underlying catalyst remained live at close.
risks_observed: Iran deal tail risk was acknowledged at entry but not hedged — a surprise diplomatic breakthrough intraday could have reversed the move sharply, and the scheduled exit provided no downside stop if that had occurred. Single-name commodity ETF concentration with no correlated short (e.g., refiner or demand-side offset) left the position fully exposed to any macro risk-off rotation that tends to hit energy alongside equities. RSI near 62 at entry left room for momentum exhaustion; a stronger reading would have flagged more crowding risk in the name.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+5.79% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Price drifted marginally higher from entry to scheduled exit, consistent with partial continuation of the prior-day gap — the weakest form of confirmation. The gain is real but thin enough that a slightly wider spread or worse fill flips it.
risks_observed: At £1.99 the bid-ask spread is a meaningful fraction of any achievable intraday move; the strategy has no cost filter to screen out low-price names where round-trip friction dominates. No intraday stop meant the full position was exposed if the reversal had been sharper.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+5.49% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Entry and exit at the same price with a negligible loss; the prior-day gain was fully absorbed at the open with zero intraday follow-through. The thesis found no expression whatsoever on the day.
risks_observed: At £0.33 the percentage spread on a round-trip likely exceeds the nominal P&L figure, meaning this trade was a net loser in real execution terms. Penny-stock names at this price level amplify all cost risks and should be filtered or position-sized differently.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+5.10% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Price declined from entry through to the scheduled exit with no recovery — prior-day momentum reversed immediately and the position absorbed the full drawdown. There was no intraday catalyst to suggest a rebound was imminent before the exit triggered.
risks_observed: RR.L is the highest-profile, most institutionally-traded name in this basket; gap-up momentum signals on large-caps are mean-reverting faster than on smaller names, and the rule applies no market-cap filter. Absence of a stop-loss meant the strategy held through the entire adverse move.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+4.48% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Price was unchanged tick-for-tick between entry and exit; the prior-day move was fully digested with no directional resolution during the holding window. The trade generated no information about whether the thesis works on Paris-listed names.
risks_observed: A EUR-denominated position in a GBP-settled book introduces FX basis that can mask real economic moves in either direction; the flat price reading may obscure a small loss after conversion costs. Capital was tied up with zero return and positive transaction cost exposure.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+4.40% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Best outcome in the basket: price moved cleanly from entry to a higher scheduled exit, with prior-day momentum extending into the session. Small-cap continuation is precisely the regime this rule is trying to exploit.
risks_observed: At £0.64 with a 1.56% gain the spread still consumes a meaningful slice of the return; a marginally worse exit would have compressed this significantly. The gain could reflect thin liquidity amplifying a small order rather than broad demand — hard to distinguish without volume data.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (GAMAl_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: Price drifted ~1% higher from entry to the scheduled exit, consistent with a mild mean-reversion recovery. The move was orderly with no intraday whipsaw implied by the clean close above entry; the thesis held in the sense that price returned toward recent range without needing a stop.
risks_observed: Reconciled entry means the original signal quality is unknown — the position could have been entered at any point in the day's range, making it impossible to assess whether the reversion trigger was well-timed. No intraday stop data means downside exposure during the hold is unverifiable.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (AEPl_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: Price slipped 5p from entry before the scheduled exit closed the position flat-to-small-loss; the expected reversion did not materialise, or the entry was already at fair value with no catalyst to pull price back. The scheduled exit prevented further drift but left no opportunity for recovery.
risks_observed: Missing original entry context makes it unclear whether this was a genuine oversold signal or a position carried from a prior session with an already-exhausted thesis; without a stop-loss floor, the position was fully exposed to any further leg lower through the hold window.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (WIXl_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: Entry and exit prices are identical at £1.73, so the P&L gain likely reflects FX conversion or a bid/ask spread advantage rather than any directional move; the stock effectively went nowhere and the scheduled exit captured noise-level positive slippage.
risks_observed: At sub-£2 penny-stock prices, the bid/ask spread is proportionally large and can dominate realised P&L in either direction; a one-tick adverse fill at entry or exit would have flipped this to a loss, and the flat price action offers no confirmation the mean-reversion signal was valid.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (IFXd_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: IFX.DE fell over 2.4% from entry to the scheduled exit with no recovery — the position moved against the thesis throughout the hold and the scheduled exit crystallised the full drawdown without any mean-reversion occurring. Given Infineon's sensitivity to semiconductor cycle sentiment, a sector-level sell-off or macro negative likely drove the sustained directional move rather than idiosyncratic noise.
risks_observed: This is the session's worst outcome and highlights the absence of a stop-loss: a 2.4% adverse move in a liquid semiconductor name is well within normal daily range for IFX.DE, meaning the strategy had no mechanism to cut before the scheduled exit. Combined with ABN.AS also losing on the day, continental European exposure contributed disproportionately to the basket's downside.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (SSPGl_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: Price ticked up one pip to exit at £1.56, producing a small gain consistent with a minimal mean-reversion in a thinly-priced stock; the move is too small to attribute to any specific driver and is within normal noise for a sub-£2 name.
risks_observed: Liquidity risk is the primary concern at this price level — small order flow can move the mid-price materially, and the reconciled entry raises questions about fill quality; the gain could easily reverse sign with a slightly different exit timing.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (PAGEl_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: PAGE.L was the session's strongest performer, gaining ~1.5% from entry to the scheduled exit; PageGroup as a recruitment-sector stock can move on macro employment data or sector rotation, and the clean upside move suggests either a genuine reversion from a prior oversold level or a day when risk appetite favoured cyclical UK mid-caps.
risks_observed: The scheduled exit left money on the table if the move had legs beyond the session, but more relevantly it also means no trailing stop captured the gain dynamically — the outcome depended entirely on the arbitrary end-of-day timing. Recruitment stocks are high-beta to economic sentiment, so the same catalyst that drove this gain could reverse sharply.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (ABNa_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: ABN AMRO dropped ~1.6% from entry to the scheduled exit with no recovery; European bank names are sensitive to rate expectations and credit spreads, and a sustained directional decline of this magnitude suggests a macro or sector headwind rather than the temporary dislocation mean-reversion targets.
risks_observed: ABN.AS and IFX.DE both lost meaningfully on the same day, indicating correlated continental European exposure that amplified the basket's downside — two of the three losers are non-UK names, which is a concentration risk if the strategy is not explicitly sizing for cross-border correlation. No stop on either position compounded the effect.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (SSEl_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: SSE gained 1.3% from entry to the scheduled exit, performing as a defensive utility would in a mixed-market session where investors rotated toward lower-beta names; the move was directional and steady rather than a sharp reversion, which may reflect sector tailwinds rather than pure mean-reversion dynamics.
risks_observed: SSE's positive performance on a day when European cyclicals (IFX.DE, ABN.AS) sold off suggests this position benefited from a flight-to-defensives dynamic that is not repeatable on mean-reversion logic alone; relying on this name as a 'safe' basket component could mask sector-rotation dependency that the strategy does not model.

thesis: Reconciled from T212 portfolio (BABl_EQ) — original ledger entry missing
outcome_notes: BAB.L ticked up ~0.8% to the scheduled exit, a modest gain consistent with mild reversion in a low-volatility defence/engineering name; the move is within normal daily range and gives no strong signal about whether the entry thesis was correct.
risks_observed: At the basket level, the session's P&L was dominated by two significant losers (IFX.DE, ABN.AS) against several small UK winners; the small gains across GAMA.L, WIX.L, SSPG.L, PAGE.L, SSE.L, and BAB.L did not fully offset the continental European drawdowns, pointing to an implicit geographic tilt in position sizing that warrants review.

thesis: Unambiguous sector leader on both 5d (+4.45%) and 20d (+8.43%) timeframes with expanding MACD histogram and healthy RSI 60; the stagflationary macro regime (oil +27.7%) is a direct, durable tailwind that argues for maximum allocation and patient hold.
outcome_notes: Price moved cleanly in the direction of the macro tailwind and the scheduled exit captured the day's drift without any intraday reversal threatening the position. The oil-driven bid held through the session, so the thesis was directionally correct and the full holding period was productive.
risks_observed: Maximum allocation to a single commodity-linked sector creates concentrated exposure to an oil price reversal; a scheduled exit rather than a signal-based one means the strategy would have held through any intraday spike-and-fade without a stop to limit drawdown. On a basket level, XLE dominated returns — if it had reversed, the other two positions were too small to offset it.

thesis: #2 on 5d and #3 on 20d with positively expanding MACD histogram and clean structure above both moving averages; stagflation environments historically rotate into consumer staples quality as real wages erode, and the defensive bid is already showing up in the data.
outcome_notes: The defensive bid materialised but was muted — the move was a fraction of XLE's, suggesting staples participated in a broad risk-on session rather than receiving a distinct flight-to-quality allocation. Scheduled exit left no room to distinguish whether this was the thesis playing out or simply beta to a rising market.
risks_observed: In a day dominated by an energy rally, XLP's correlation to the broader tape means the defensive rationale was not actually tested; a down-tape session is the only valid test of whether the defensive rotation thesis adds independent value. Holding both XLE and XLP simultaneously creates a mild cross-current — one is an inflation beneficiary, the other an inflation hedge — and the portfolio logic breaks down if the macro regime shifts quickly.

thesis: MACD histogram turning up from negative territory (+0.286) while 5d momentum ranks 4th; the macro 'Mildly Bullish' view on defensive rotation into healthcare quality provides a modest but aligned edge — sized smaller given below-SMA50 technical overhang.
outcome_notes: The position was essentially flat — the MACD histogram turn-up produced no meaningful price follow-through on the day, consistent with the below-SMA50 technical overhang suppressing momentum. The smaller sizing was appropriate given the weak signal, but the trade contributed negligible edge.
risks_observed: A position entered below SMA50 with a nascent MACD recovery signal is at risk of being a premature mean-reversion bet disguised as a momentum trade; one more weak session would have invalidated the histogram signal entirely before the scheduled exit. The near-zero contribution also highlights a basket construction risk: three simultaneous scheduled exits create no differentiation between a thesis that confirmed and one that simply didn't move.

thesis: Publicis Groupe announced an all-cash acquisition of LiveRamp via a definitive merger agreement filed in an 8-K today (May 18); stock is trading at M&A arb discount to likely deal price (~$40) with 8x average volume confirming sustained institutional positioning in deal spread.
outcome_notes: The M&A spread failed to compress intraday — the stock drifted fractionally below entry and held its discount to the ~$40 deal price through close. High volume confirmed institutional awareness of the spread, but that positioning was already reflected in the opening price, leaving no intraday compression to capture on a same-day exit.
risks_observed: The thesis is structurally a multi-week arb, not an intraday catalyst play; a scheduled same-day exit is mismatched to the convergence timeline of a definitive merger agreement. Deal-break risk (regulatory, financing, competing bidder absence) is also unhedged, and any adverse headline would gap the stock well below entry with no stop in place.

thesis: WSJ reported today that Elliott Management built a large activist stake in Bio-Rad; activist involvement is a strong and fresh catalyst for strategic action and multiple expansion, with RSI 55.8 leaving significant room before overbought conditions.
outcome_notes: The Elliott activist disclosure drove a clean, sustained bid through the session with no meaningful intraday reversal before the scheduled exit. Price action was consistent with institutional accumulation continuing after the initial news, validating the catalyst quality.
risks_observed: Activist-stake disclosures can be front-run by prime brokerage desks before public reporting, so entry at open may already be late relative to the initial move. If the position is part of a basket with other activist-catalyst names, correlation spikes on broad risk-off days could compress the spread before strategic action materialises.

thesis: BofA Securities reinstated ServiceNow as Buy with a $130 price target today (29% implied upside), stock is above both key moving averages with elevated relative volume of 0.65 suggesting institutional follow-through on the fresh analyst catalyst.
outcome_notes: The BofA reinstatement generated consistent follow-through buying into close, with the stock moving ~1.9% — the large implied upside in the price target drew momentum participants alongside fundamental buyers. Exit at close captured the full intraday move without reversion.
risks_observed: Analyst reinstatements with wide price targets are frequently anticipated by sell-side desks ahead of publication, meaning a portion of the move may have already occurred before the open entry. With a scheduled exit and no stop, any intraday reversal — e.g., from a competing downgrade or sector sell-off — would have been held through in full.

thesis: Benchmark raised Intel's price target to $140 today alongside Trump publicly signaling political support for Intel investment; after a -17.6% 5-day pullback from a massive +62% 20-day run, the stock has reset to a more favorable entry above both MAs.
outcome_notes: The stock was flat on the session despite two fresh catalysts, with the price target raise from a secondary broker and political commentary failing to attract net buyers on the day. Post-run distribution pressure from the prior +62% move appears to have fully absorbed the catalyst flow.
risks_observed: Entering after a parabolic 20-day run into a Benchmark (not tier-1) price target raise carries significant overhead supply risk; the thesis required momentum continuation but the prior move likely exhausted near-term buyers. Political signals are unreliable as price catalysts with no defined timeline, introducing further uncertainty about when or whether the thesis would resolve.

thesis: RSI 27.5 and -31.66% 20-day decline while Citigroup maintains a Buy rating signals a technical selloff, not fundamental deterioration — analyst conviction argues the market has overshot. Price at $17.42 vs $22.07 20-day MA implies ~27% reversion to mean.
outcome_notes: Price nudged up 11 cents before the scheduled exit — directionally correct but capturing only a sliver of the ~27% MA gap. No catalyst emerged to accelerate the move, so the Citigroup Buy rating provided a sentiment floor rather than a lift; the thesis held in direction but the hold window was far too short to express a reversion of that magnitude.
risks_observed: A 27% gap to the 20-day MA implies a multi-week recovery horizon that a same-session scheduled exit structurally cannot capture — the mismatch between thesis timeframe and exit rule is the primary inefficiency here. Medical device names at this discount also carry latent binary risk (reimbursement rulings, trial readouts) that a clean RSI oversold signal does not screen for.

thesis: RSI 22.6 with zero news accompanying a -24% 5-day and -29% 20-day move — price is 25% below the 20-day MA with no fundamental reason visible. Textbook technical exhaustion; mean-reversion target is the ~$36–38 MA band.
outcome_notes: Price fell a further 34 cents through the hold, meaning sellers were not exhausted despite the extreme RSI reading. The 'no visible news' framing proved insufficient — either institutional distribution or index-related flow was driving continuation that technical exhaustion signals failed to flag.
risks_observed: A -24%/-29% 5/20-day decline with no public catalyst is a stronger signal of supply overhang than of panic selling; the exhaustion thesis required the selling to be emotional, not structural. The scheduled-exit-only structure had no floor: a deeper continuation trade would have compounded the loss unchecked, and REZI represented the basket's highest open-ended downside exposure.

thesis: RSI 24.2 with no accompanying news after a -20% 20-day decline in a stable auto parts distribution business. Price at $24.67 vs $28.88 20-day MA offers a well-defined ~17% bounce target with no fundamental trigger for the move down.
outcome_notes: Price drifted up 6 cents — essentially flat relative to the 17% MA gap the thesis targeted. Auto parts distribution is low-volatility by nature; absent a specific catalyst, mean-reversion in this sector plays out over days to weeks, not hours, so the scheduled exit captured noise rather than signal.
risks_observed: LKQ and CCS share indirect rate-cycle sensitivity through consumer credit and housing turnover, making them more correlated within this basket than their separate sector labels suggest. The near-zero gain came from bid-ask drift, not directional movement, which raises the question of whether transaction costs net this trade to a loss at execution-quality granularity.

thesis: RSI 28.9 and -23% 20-day selloff with no company-specific news — homebuilder weakness is macro rate-fear driven, not CCS-specific. Price at $48.60 vs $55.43 20-day MA provides a clean reversion target once rate panic subsides.
outcome_notes: Price slipped 13 cents, confirming that macro rate pressure on homebuilders continued through the hold window without pause. The thesis required rate panic to abate — it didn't within the session — and the scheduled exit gave no time for that condition to change.
risks_observed: CCS and REZI together concentrate rate-beta risk in the same basket: one a homebuilder, the other HVAC and smart-home, both sensitive to the same rate-fear regime driving the selloffs. Neither position had a stop, so a sustained rate move could have extended both losses simultaneously; the basket carried this correlation without any hedge or sizing adjustment.

thesis: RSI 26.5 oversold in a defensive consumer staple; analyst maintained Equal-Weight (no downgrade) after a -17% 20-day move, confirming no fundamental change. Flowers Foods' predictable business profile and the best volume ratio (0.44) among the deep-oversold names make this the cleanest execution setup.
outcome_notes: Price moved a single cent — the defensive staple's low intraday volatility meant no reversion trigger materialised within the hold window, and no deterioration occurred either. The 'cleanest setup' characterisation held in the sense that it didn't trade against the position, but scheduled exit captured nothing of the underlying thesis.
risks_observed: At a $7.32 handle, the bid-ask spread consumes a disproportionate share of any small gain, making transaction cost drag a structural drag that the P&L number likely flatters. Held alongside REZI and CCS — both with sharper loss potential — FLO's low-volatility profile offers no basket-level offset; it adds neither hedge nor uncorrelated return.

thesis: Energy is the top macro call for W21 — oil +27.7% (20d) in a stagflationary regime directly inflates offshore drilling day rates, and VAL as a pure-play deepwater/harsh-environment operator is the cleanest expression of sustained high oil prices. RSI 70.3 above both SMAs with positive MACD and +25.79% 20d momentum confirm the sector call is being priced in at the stock level.
outcome_notes: VAL held its upward trajectory through the scheduled exit, with the oil macro tailwind continuing to support offshore drillers without meaningful reversal. The scheduled exit captured the move cleanly — no stop pressure, no thesis deterioration, just trend continuation.
risks_observed: At RSI 70+ on entry, any oil sentiment reversal or inventory surprise could have triggered a sharp mean-reversion in an already extended name. Single-commodity dependency means VAL has near-zero diversification value within an energy-heavy basket.

thesis: Healthcare is rated mildly bullish as defensive rotation re-asserts with quality premium attractive in an uncertain stagflationary regime; BIO has the added stock-level catalyst of Elliott Management building a large activist stake announced today, providing institutional support and a catalyst for operational improvements from a mid-50s RSI that is not yet overbought.
outcome_notes: BIO was the strongest performer in the basket, with the Elliott activist catalyst providing a durable bid that carried through the holding period. Price action confirmed institutional accumulation rather than a fade, validating the event-driven overlay on top of the sector rotation call.
risks_observed: Activist-driven moves can unwind abruptly if the stake is leaked to be smaller than reported, hedged, or if Elliott signals disengagement — the catalyst is binary and not sector-correlated, making it harder to hedge. Sizing this alongside macro defensives creates a mixed-signal position that behaves differently from the rest of the healthcare allocation under stress.

thesis: Technology sector is mildly bullish and oil-price surge creates a secondary narrative tailwind for distributed solar as an alternative-energy beneficiary; ENPH shows the strongest technical confirmation of any Tech candidate with RSI 71.5, +32.72% 5d and +47.49% 20d above both SMAs, with no company-specific negative news to fade the move.
outcome_notes: ENPH effectively went nowhere, with the exit nearly flat despite the strong technical setup on entry — the oil-as-solar-tailwind narrative did not translate into incremental buying during the holding period. The position was dead weight: extended technicals stalled rather than resolved higher.
risks_observed: Entering a name at RSI 71.5 and +47% 20d momentum with a narrative-driven rather than fundamental thesis is a timing risk — momentum exhaustion is the base case, not the edge case. The indirect relationship between oil prices and ENPH demand is too weak a catalyst to sustain a breakout from an already overbought level, and this trade flagged that risk clearly in hindsight.

thesis: Iran nuclear talks explicitly stalling — White House rejected today's proposal as insufficient, keeping Hormuz disruption risk elevated with no near-term resolution pathway. Oil up 24% in 20 days on confirmed supply shock; RSI 63, positive MACD, and price 7.7% above SMA20 confirm the uptrend is intact and unextended.
outcome_notes: Price drifted lower from entry to a scheduled exit, with the geopolitical catalyst failing to sustain intraday momentum — likely a case of 'buy the rumor, sell the news' dynamics as the market had already priced the Iran stall. The exit was time-driven rather than signal-driven, so the position was closed regardless of whether the thesis remained live.
risks_observed: A 24% run-up over 20 days meant significant speculative premium was already embedded in the price, making the entry vulnerable to any softening in sentiment even without a fundamental reversal. Scheduled exits remove discretion at precisely the moment when holding or cutting early might be warranted — if the geopolitical driver is still active, a time-based rule captures none of that upside; if it's fading, it holds too long.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+7.90% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: BL held its prior-day momentum into the session, advancing a further 1.4% before the scheduled exit. Price action was constructive — no meaningful giveback — suggesting the gap was supported by genuine buying rather than fading enthusiasm.
risks_observed: A single-day momentum filter with no volume or catalyst check means entries like this are indistinguishable from ones that reverse sharply; the win here was directional luck. Three of five names in the basket closed lower, so basket-level correlation to mean-reversion risk was high on the day.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+7.57% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: ABR faded from entry, closing the session below the buy price — the prior-day gap was not sustained, consistent with a low-float name where the opening surge exhausted demand. The scheduled exit captured the full intraday drift lower without any stop intervening.
risks_observed: ABR at ~$6 carries liquidity and spread risk that higher-priced names in the same basket don't; equal-notional sizing effectively overweights it in share terms and amplifies slippage exposure. No intraday stop meant the loss accumulated unimpeded.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+7.50% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: NSP drifted lower through the session, unwinding roughly a point from entry before the scheduled close. The prior-day gap failed to attract follow-through buyers, and price action was soft without a clear catalyst to sustain it.
risks_observed: NSP is a mid-cap HR-services name whose gaps are often driven by earnings or guidance; entering without filtering for whether the move was catalyst-driven introduces the risk of buying exhausted post-event supply. The loss, while modest, was entirely preventable with a basic catalyst check.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+6.85% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: FDS gave back 1.5% from entry to the scheduled exit, with price declining steadily — the prior session's gain was not confirmed by further buying on this day. No news or sector catalyst was visible to explain the reversal, suggesting the gap simply attracted profit-taking.
risks_observed: At ~$227 per share, FDS carries the highest per-share price in the basket, meaning execution slippage and bid-ask spread are less punishing in percentage terms, but the absolute dollar loss is amplified if the position is sized on notional rather than volatility. The basket ran 3-of-5 losers today, indicating a systematic mean-reversion headwind for the strategy on this date.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+6.75% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: FICO extended its prior-day move, adding 0.73% to the scheduled exit — the gap held and buyers remained engaged through the session. As the highest-priced name in the basket, even a small percentage gain produced a positive contribution.
risks_observed: FICO's elevated absolute price (~$1,168) means that equal-notional position sizing results in very few shares, concentrating execution risk around the open and close where wide spreads can meaningfully erode a sub-1% gain. The strategy has no mechanism to distinguish this continuation from the reversals seen in ABR, NSP, and FDS on the same day.

thesis: Energy is the macro regime's primary beneficiary — USO at 149, supply shock intact, no demand-destruction signal. EXV3.DE confirms with +3.76%/+5.46% 5d/20d momentum, RSI 60.8, and price above both SMA20 and SMA50, directly mirroring the XLE leadership at the top of the US sector rankings.
outcome_notes: The energy thesis held: price advanced intraday and the position closed modestly green, consistent with continued supply-shock tailwinds and XLE's sector leadership. The scheduled exit captured only a fraction of the available move — no trailing mechanism was in place to let a confirming trend run.
risks_observed: Energy was the sole green leg in the basket, masking the defensive side's underperformance; a correlated commodity or geopolitical reversal intraday could have flipped the whole book negative simultaneously. With no intraday stop on the position, a sharp energy reversal after entry would have had no circuit-breaker.

thesis: Healthcare is the defensive quality pick in a risk-off, rising-real-rates environment — mildly bullish in macro view, EXH1.DE shows +2.79%/+4.38% momentum across both timeframes with clean structure above SMA20 and SMA50, providing portfolio ballast if the Tech crack deepens.
outcome_notes: Healthcare failed to function as ballast — price drifted lower through the session and the scheduled exit realised a small loss, suggesting the risk-off defensive bid was either absent or not strong enough to offset mild selling pressure in the name. The clean MA structure at entry did not translate into sustained buying.
risks_observed: Both defensive legs (EXH1.DE and EXH5.DE) lost ground on the same day, indicating the 'defensive rotation' thesis was correlation risk disguised as diversification — a single-day risk-off reversal or sector-wide ETF outflow would have hit both simultaneously. The absence of any stop meant the loss was capped only by the scheduled exit, not by price action.

thesis: Consumer Staples (Food & Beverage) leads the candidate set on 5d returns (+3.90%) and is the defensive rotation trade aligned with the macro stagflationary backdrop; above both MAs with a positive MACD, it rounds out a concentrated Energy + Defensives portfolio that avoids every macro-bearish sector.
outcome_notes: Despite leading the candidate set on recent momentum, Consumer Staples reversed intraday and the position closed underwater, suggesting the prior 5d strength was mean-reverting rather than a breakout continuation. The scheduled exit prevented further drawdown but offered no active management of the deteriorating tape.
risks_observed: The basket ran three positions with only one net winner, and the two losers were in adjacent defensive sub-sectors that moved in lockstep — the portfolio's effective sector diversification was lower than the ticker count implied. Momentum-at-entry as the primary signal carries look-back bias risk; a single strong 5d window can reflect an already-exhausted move rather than an ongoing one.

thesis: Cleanest momentum trade in the list — Infineon's +11%/+34.5% 5d/20d returns, non-overbought RSI (65.9), and strongly positive MACD reflect genuine demand for power semiconductors in AI data-centre buildouts; stop set at ~2x ATR below close, target at ~3x ATR.
outcome_notes: Price moved against the position before recovering to the exit level, suggesting the stop was either triggered or the system exited on the partial recovery rather than holding for target. The AI data-centre demand narrative remained intact structurally, but the near-term action implies either profit-taking off the recent run or broader semiconductor sector rotation on the day — not a thesis invalidation.
risks_observed: The +34.5% 20-day move meant significant embedded gains in the float; any macro wobble or sector rotation can flush late momentum entrants even when the thesis is sound. The missing entry price ($0.00) is a data integrity gap that makes it impossible to verify whether the stop was sized correctly relative to realised volatility on the day.

thesis: ABN AMRO just beat Q1 forecasts with analyst upgrades following; +11% 5d with accelerating MACD histogram and yield curve steepening providing a structural NIM tailwind for European bank margins — fundamental catalyst reinforcing the technical trend.
outcome_notes: The trade exited at a loss despite the price recovering, indicating the recovery was incomplete — the exit was taken on the bounce rather than at breakeven or target. European bank momentum is highly sensitive to intraday macro signals (rate prints, ECB commentary), and any reversal in short-end rates or credit spreads on the day would have immediately pressured NIM-driven names regardless of the Q1 catalyst.
risks_observed: All three basket positions were in trending, recently strong sectors simultaneously — semiconductors, banks, and consumer staples — meaning a broad risk-off intraday move would hit all legs at once with no natural hedge. ABN specifically adds FX exposure (EUR/GBP) that is unaccounted for in the exit price, which may have contributed to the sterling-denominated loss even if the local price recovered.

thesis: British American Tobacco is the clearest expression of the macro view's Consumer Staples defensive bid — +17% 20d, above both MAs, strong MACD momentum; position sized below max given RSI at 70.0 approaching overbought.
outcome_notes: RSI was flagged at exactly 70.0 at entry, and the recovered exit with a loss is consistent with a brief overbought pullback that failed to fully retrace — the defensive/staples bid provided some cushion but not enough to prevent a negative close. The smaller loss relative to the other two legs suggests the reduced position sizing on overbought concern functioned as intended.
risks_observed: At RSI 70.0, any catalyst for profit-taking (currency move, sector rotation into risk-on) creates asymmetric downside for late momentum entries; the strategy has no mechanism to distinguish between a healthy consolidation and a trend reversal at that signal level. Basket-level, all three trades sharing a 'recovered but still negative' outcome on the same date points to a common intraday driver — worth checking whether a single macro event (e.g. USD/EUR move, risk-off spike) explains the simultaneous drawdown across uncorrelated sectors.

thesis: Short-duration gilts are the only technically clean name in this universe — above both MAs with positive 5d momentum. In a stagflationary 'no cuts' regime, short duration minimises rate-path risk while maintaining bond exposure; yield anchoring at the short end keeps carry stable.
outcome_notes: Price returned exactly to entry by the scheduled exit — no directional move materialized in either direction, leaving transaction friction as the sole P&L driver. The technical setup held (no breakdown), but the 'no cuts' regime produced stasis rather than the expected yield-anchoring bid on the short end.
risks_observed: A technically clean instrument in a low-volatility session becomes a pure friction trade; the setup offered no margin to recover spread costs without intraday momentum. Scheduled exit removed any ability to hold for a late-session catalyst, making the hold period the binding constraint rather than the thesis.

thesis: HY credit is the one constructive macro signal: HY outperformed IG by 62bp last week and IHYG holds above SMA50, confirming spreads are not widening. This is a credit-resilience trade, not a duration trade — sized smaller given thin volume and tighter take-profit to reflect the limited upside in a stagflationary backdrop.
outcome_notes: No price movement from entry to exit — the prior week's HY outperformance signal had already been absorbed, and no incremental spread compression occurred within the holding window. The thin-volume flag proved relevant: the effective spread likely accounts for the bulk of the loss on an otherwise flat print.
risks_observed: Both basket legs closed negative on flat prices, pointing to a session where transaction costs dominate and no strategy recovers friction without directional follow-through. HY in a stagflationary backdrop also carries asymmetric gap risk on any sudden risk-off print — a smaller position helps but doesn't eliminate that tail, and there was no stop to contain it intraday.

thesis: Confirmed $3.875bn divestiture of Australian steelmaking coal assets is a hard M&A catalyst completing Anglo's portfolio simplification ahead of Teck merger; analyst fair value revised to £36.53 and ESG-driven re-rating thesis has further to run as restricted capital returns.
outcome_notes: Price drifted below entry throughout the hold and exited at the scheduled time with no recovery. The divestiture announcement was already digested by the market at entry — notably, the revised analyst fair value of £36.53 sat below the entry price of £37.07, leaving no valuation buffer for the thesis to play out.
risks_observed: Entry was priced above the freshly revised fair value, so the risk/reward was inverted from the start. The ESG re-rating and capital returns angle is a multi-week catalyst at best; in a scheduled short-hold structure it generated no price support, and there was no secondary trigger to force a re-rate within the window.

thesis: Yesterday's H2 earnings confirmed return to growth with higher sales, revenue and backlog after a prolonged contraction — reaction is fresh (one day old) and analyst model updates should provide continued tailwind above both SMAs.
outcome_notes: Price was essentially flat at exit, confirming that the earnings-driven momentum had already exhausted itself before entry. Anticipated analyst model updates either didn't arrive during the hold or had no visible price impact — the stock simply consolidated rather than continuing higher.
risks_observed: Entering a full day after the initial reaction means the reactive leg had already run; the position was exposed to post-event drift with no fresh catalyst to sustain it. The near-zero move is the benign outcome — the same timing lag that produced a -0.18% loss here could produce a sharper mean-reversion if the tape turns.

thesis: Confirmed earnings beat (May 17) with shares up 3.6% on results day and drift continuing; post-earnings analyst revisions are still flowing and technicals above both SMAs support further appreciation.
outcome_notes: The stop was triggered, meaning post-earnings drift reversed rather than continued — the earnings beat had been fully absorbed two days prior and offered no ongoing buying pressure. The stop exit at £2.01 against a £2.09 entry implies the stock broke back below the post-results support level, invalidating the technical leg of the thesis.
risks_observed: At this price and size, bid-ask spread and thin liquidity likely widened the effective loss versus the theoretical stop level. The -4.00% drawdown is the largest in today's basket and points to the stop being placed too close to a volatile, low-priced name where normal intraday noise can clip a position; a volatility-adjusted stop or wider hold tolerance is needed for names in this price tier.

thesis: Cleanest mean-reversion setup: RSI 29.3 (below 30 threshold), price 1.9 ATRs below the 20-day MA at £1.30, and no news-driven catalyst for the -16.8% 20-day decline. Low volume today suggests seller exhaustion rather than continued distribution.
outcome_notes: Price did recover enough to trigger the 'recovered' exit, but the bounce was shallow — the -16.8% 20-day decline absorbed the recovery before price could meaningfully close the gap to SMA20. Low-volume exhaustion read was directionally correct but timing was off; seller pressure persisted into the session.
risks_observed: An entry at the RSI 30 threshold is still well above capitulation territory for a name with a 16.8% drawdown — the 'exhaustion' signal was ambiguous. No stop-loss is visible in the structure, so the position was entirely dependent on the recovered trigger firing before further deterioration.

thesis: Extreme RSI 23.7 with price 3.4 ATRs below SMA20 and no fundamental news to explain the -25.76% 20-day decline; very low volume today (0.13x average) points to technical exhaustion. Smaller allocation accounts for the unusual severity of the decline.
outcome_notes: The 'recovered' exit fired but the loss was the largest in the basket, consistent with a 3.4 ATR extension that rarely snaps back in a single holding period. The 0.13x volume read as exhaustion, but thin volume can also mean no buyers — price drifted rather than bounced.
risks_observed: A 25.76% 20-day decline at RSI 23.7 is well outside normal mean-reversion territory; the reduced allocation acknowledged severity but the setup was closer to a falling-knife than the others. Correlation with other UK small/mid names in the basket amplified downside if any macro catalyst hit the session.

thesis: RSI 35.2 with price 1.8 ATRs below SMA20 and no news catalyst for the -10.76% 20-day pullback; MACD histogram negative but RSI at levels that historically precede mean-reversion bounces.
outcome_notes: The shallowest setup in the basket (RSI 35.2, 1.8 ATRs) produced the smallest loss, suggesting the signal was calibrated correctly relative to the other entries — the recovery path was cleaner with less extension to unwind. Exit on 'recovered' captured a real, if modest, bounce.
risks_observed: RSI 35.2 is borderline for this strategy's threshold and the MACD histogram still negative at entry means momentum had not yet turned — the trade relied entirely on RSI reversion without confirming price action. Liquidity in MGNS.L may be thin enough that the exit itself moved the price.

thesis: RSI 36.8 and 2.2 ATRs below SMA20 with no company-specific negative news; defensive utility characteristics reduce falling-knife risk relative to cyclical names, making this a lower-risk mean-reversion entry.
outcome_notes: The defensive utility framing held in the sense that the loss was contained and the recovered exit fired, but SSE's 2.2 ATR extension didn't fully close — the bounce was real but not enough to overcome entry friction. Sector characteristics limited downside without accelerating the upside.
risks_observed: Utility names carry regulatory and rate-sensitivity risk that RSI/ATR models don't capture; a rate-driven selloff looks identical to a mean-reversion setup but doesn't reverse on technicals alone. The basket held four UK-listed names simultaneously, concentrating currency and UK-macro exposure.

thesis: RSI 35.1 and 2.2 ATRs below SMA20 with no stock-specific bad news; the -11% 20-day decline appears sector/macro-driven (tariff overhang) rather than fundamental deterioration, making a technical bounce plausible as sentiment stabilises.
outcome_notes: The tariff overhang identified in the thesis remained live — sentiment did not stabilise on the day, so the technical bounce was weak and the recovered trigger caught only a shallow move. A macro-driven decline in an auto name rarely reverses on intraday technicals unless the macro catalyst itself resolves.
risks_observed: Entering a EUR-denominated name into a GBP P&L book adds FX drag that isn't reflected in the RSI/ATR signal; any EUR weakness on the day compounded the loss. Sector-level tariff risk is a regime shift, not a reversion candidate — this setup had higher false-signal probability than the domestic UK names in the same basket.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+14.25% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Price drifted higher through the session and closed near the day's range top, suggesting the large prior-day gap (14.25%) had genuine institutional follow-through rather than noise. The scheduled exit captured that continuation cleanly.
risks_observed: A 14.25% overnight gap in a large-cap name is an extreme outlier and statistically more likely to mean-revert; the win here was not representative of the expected distribution. No stop was in place to protect against the more probable fade scenario.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+11.24% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Momentum from an 11.24% gap carried intraday, and price closed above entry without significant drawdown. The move likely reflected a fundamental catalyst (pharma is prone to news-driven gaps) that retained buying interest through the session.
risks_observed: Pharma gap-ups following news events often see sharp reversals once the initial reaction is digested; holding through a scheduled exit with no stop exposed the position to that tail. Correlation with LSEG — both large-cap gainers on the same day — meant two meaningful positions were exposed to the same macro sentiment shift.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+7.75% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Price faded steadily from entry and closed below it, the prior-day gap giving way to profit-taking. The +7.75% prior move was the weakest in the basket and in a small-cap miner, where gaps are more likely driven by thin liquidity than sustained demand.
risks_observed: Small-cap mining names carry outsized spread and liquidity risk; the effective cost of entry and exit erodes a position at this price level faster than the nominal P&L shows. No intraday stop meant the full -3.12% drawdown was absorbed without any decision gate.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+5.38% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: The gap reversed almost immediately and the position was underwater for most of the session, closing at the low end of the day's range. At a sub-£3.10 price point, the prior +5.38% gain was likely thin-book noise rather than a signal of directional momentum.
risks_observed: This is the largest percentage loss in the basket and the lowest-priced name — spread costs and price impact on entry/exit are disproportionate. Three of the five positions (ATYM, SHAW, BCG) ended negative, all sub-£10 price points, which suggests a systematic bias: the no-filter rule is selecting illiquid, reverting names alongside genuine movers.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+4.81% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Price slipped from entry and never recovered, following the same fade pattern as ATYM and SHAW. The +4.81% prior-day gain — smallest in the basket — did not persist, consistent with a low-float, noise-driven move.
risks_observed: At end-of-day basket level, the two profitable trades (both large-cap, both >11% prior-day gaps) were outweighed by three small-cap fades, resulting in a net-negative session despite a 40% win rate by count. The strategy has no size, liquidity, or market-cap filter, so position sizing is identical across names with very different risk profiles — a structural issue more than a one-day observation.

thesis: Oil supply shock thesis is intact — Iran military standoff maintains a risk premium regardless of Monday's tactical delay, and USO's technicals confirm active momentum (above SMA20/50, RSI 62, +23% 20d). Wider stop accommodates intraday diplomatic headline volatility without shaking out on noise.
outcome_notes: USO drifted steadily higher into the scheduled exit, consistent with a market that continued to price geopolitical risk premium without a resolution catalyst. Price action was constructive — no sharp retracements that would have tested the wider stop — suggesting the momentum signal rather than any single headline drove the gain. Exit was time-based, not triggered by thesis invalidation.
risks_observed: A single-name crude proxy concentrates headline risk: any unexpected de-escalation (ceasefire signal, diplomatic statement) could have reversed the premium sharply intraday, and a scheduled exit offers no protection against that gap. With no correlated hedges in the basket, a broad risk-off commodity sell-off would have hit the position without an offset. Hindsight flag: RSI at 62 on entry left limited buffer before overbought territory, compressing the asymmetry of the trade.

thesis: Dominant sector leader by 5d and 20d relative strength, confirmed by MACD histogram expansion, above both SMAs, and a structurally intact oil supply shock thesis with WTI at $106. Wider take-profit to allow the multi-day energy rotation to mature.
outcome_notes: The energy rotation thesis held — price moved cleanly in the direction of the prevailing oil supply shock narrative and closed above entry. Exit was time-driven rather than take-profit triggered, so the wider TP target was never tested; the move matured but didn't exhaust itself before the scheduled close.
risks_observed: A scheduled exit on a thesis with explicit multi-day duration means the strategy systematically caps gains before the catalyst resolves — if WTI continued higher after close, the sizing advantage of the wider TP was wasted. Concentration in a single commodity-linked ETF leaves the position fully exposed to an intraday oil reversal with no trailing stop to defend accumulated gains.

thesis: Secondary sector leader in a stagflationary regime; above both SMAs with positive MACD and RSI at 60 — defensive pricing power bid is intact as real rates rise and the soft-landing narrative erodes.
outcome_notes: The defensive bid didn't materialise within the holding window — price drifted marginally below entry and the scheduled exit captured a small loss. Staples underperforming while energy gained ~1.3% on the same day suggests risk appetite remained tilted toward cyclicals, undermining the stagflationary rotation premise in the near term.
risks_observed: Running XLP long alongside XLE in the same basket creates an implicit hedge that dilutes overall directional conviction — energy and defensives rarely lead simultaneously, so one leg is structurally likely to drag. The absence of any stop on XLP meant the position relied entirely on the scheduled exit as its only downside control, and the basket-level correlation between these two legs was not explicitly accounted for in sizing.

thesis: RSI 24.7 with four major sell-side desks (JPM, BMO, Truist, Canaccord) all maintaining Buy/Outperform — PT cuts reflect model resets, not a change in view. The 16.6% 20-day decline looks like sector-rotation selling into a quality large-cap biotech, a high-probability technical mean-reversion.

thesis: Agilysys filed an 8-K on May 18 reporting a Q4 double beat plus FY27 revenue guidance above analyst estimates, the archetypal earnings-catalyst setup for this strategy. Volume is running at 1.28× the 20-day average today, confirming the market is still absorbing the news rather than already fully pricing it; Needham reiterates Buy at $120 (32% above current price), providing analyst-backed upside runway.
outcome_notes: Price sold off through the stop rather than holding post-earnings support, indicating the initial gap or pop had already been absorbed before entry and there was no sustained follow-through from buyers. The double-beat catalyst failed to produce lasting demand, likely meaning the news was priced in on May 18 and the May 19 session represented distribution rather than continued accumulation.
risks_observed: Entering a news-driven setup on the day after the 8-K filing rather than the same session exposed this trade to the common pattern of second-day fade once overnight momentum traders exit. Volume at 1.28× average was elevated but not conclusively strong, and sole reliance on a single analyst reiteration (rather than a wave of upgrades) left the upside case thin; no apparent catalyst to absorb selling pressure once the stop was tested.

thesis: Agilysys delivered a Q4 double-beat with above-consensus FY27 revenue guidance, driving above-average volume (1.29×) and confirming genuine institutional demand rather than thin-tape drift; Needham reiterates Buy at $120 (34% above close) providing a credible upside anchor. Hospitality software is insulated from the AI-capex-durability concerns dragging semiconductor names, and the earnings catalyst in a risk-off tape marks it as a relative-strength outlier.
outcome_notes: Post-earnings momentum failed to hold beyond the entry session; the broader risk-off tape overwhelmed the single-stock catalyst and price sold through the stop without any intervening recovery. Volume confirmation at entry did not translate into sustained institutional support — the signal described conditions at the open, not over the hold period.
risks_observed: Entering a small/mid-cap software name immediately after an earnings gap concentrates risk at the worst liquidity point: stops on thin-float names in a down tape can gap rather than fill cleanly. The 'insulated from AI-capex concerns' framing was a relative argument that assumed broad sector rotation into quality software, which did not materialise on the day.

thesis: ServiceNow's enterprise workflow automation demand is driven by AI-driven efficiency spend — a more durable capex line than infrastructure buildout — separating it from the AMAT/MU/ORCL capex-durability selloff; the stock is above both SMAs with a strong MACD positive crossover after a +22.67% 5-day run. The 65% cash allocation held back reflects the absence of any Energy, Staples, or Healthcare setup that meets both the macro alignment and technical quality bar simultaneously.
outcome_notes: Price drifted lower through the scheduled hold period with no fresh catalyst to extend the prior 5-day run; the scheduled exit captured the loss rather than a stop cutting it earlier. The AI-efficiency-spend differentiation thesis was directionally reasonable but the entry followed exhausted momentum, and no incremental news arrived to re-engage buyers.
risks_observed: A scheduled exit with no stop meant full downside exposure to drift for the entire hold — asymmetric on the loss side relative to the upside available after a +22% move. Sector-level multiple compression in enterprise software during a risk-off tape pulled the name lower regardless of fundamental differentiation, exposing a correlation assumption that was not stress-tested in the position sizing.

thesis: Oil supply shock thesis intact and intensifying — Iran military activity and G7 sanctions push add fresh geopolitical premium on top of a technically strong trend (RSI 63.7, above SMA20 by 7%, +17.89% 20d). Expected ~+2.1% day return clears the 0.30% round-trip cost gate by a wide margin; the only commodity with macro, technical, and news alignment today.
outcome_notes: Price moved in the anticipated direction but delivered roughly half the expected day return (+1.11% vs ~+2.1% thesis). The scheduled exit captured a clean intraday grind higher, suggesting the geopolitical premium bid was real but partially priced in at entry; no sharp catalyst materialised intraday to accelerate the move.
risks_observed: Single-name commodity exposure with no hedge meant any reversal in geopolitical sentiment (ceasefire signal, sanctions walkback) would have hit the full position. The trade also sat at the top of a 7% SMA20 extension — momentum overshoot risk was present even if it didn't trigger. No stop was cited, so downside on a sentiment reversal was open-ended within the session.

thesis: Textbook post-earnings momentum entry: Q4 beat with strong FY27 revenue guidance, volume at 1.40x average confirming institutional buying, Needham reiterating Buy at $120 (~32% above current). The only candidate with all momentum signals aligned — catalyst, volume, trend, and analyst support simultaneously present.
outcome_notes: Price failed to hold post-earnings gains and reversed sharply through the stop, suggesting the initial volume spike was distribution rather than accumulation. The entry thesis did not hold — institutional buying did not sustain, and the Needham price target provided no near-term price support as sellers dominated.
risks_observed: Single-name concentration with no correlated hedge meant the full drawdown landed on one position; the 1.40x volume confirmation proved ambiguous in hindsight — elevated volume on a beat can signal both entry and exit by larger holders. A wider stop relative to the earnings-day range would have been stopped out regardless, flagging that earnings-momentum entries carry gap-risk that standard stop placement underestimates.

outcome_notes: Price was unchanged from entry to scheduled exit — the prior-day momentum stalled entirely with no intraday follow-through. The net loss is attributable purely to transaction costs and FX conversion, confirming the thesis produced zero edge on this name.
risks_observed: A flat-price outcome still generates a guaranteed loss once spread and FX friction are included, meaning the hurdle rate for this strategy is higher than it appears from raw P&L percentages. No intraday exit mechanism meant dead capital was held through the full session.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+5.02% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
outcome_notes: Price declined steadily from entry through the scheduled exit, with the prior-day gap acting as a supply overhang rather than a springboard. Mean-reversion dynamics dominated — the open attracted sellers unwinding the previous session's move rather than new buyers extending it.
risks_observed: No stop-loss in place meant the full 2.65% drawdown was absorbed passively. YELP and BKNG both lost on the same day in consumer-facing sectors, indicating correlated exposure within the basket that amplified the session's damage.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+4.66% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.

risks_observed: A low-priced, lower-liquidity name is susceptible to wide intraday spreads that can silently erode realised P&L; the reported gain may flatter the true execution quality. The win here also partially masked that the basket was net negative on the day.

thesis: Top previous-day gainer (+4.56% close-to-open). Rule-based control: buying the prior session's strongest movers with no further filter.
